seo.title: Healthcare RAG Pipeline AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for rag pipeline AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying a RAG pipeline in healthcare environments introduces compounding risks: retrieval errors can surface outdated clinical guidance, LLM hallucinations can fabricate drug interactions or dosing information, and any PHI exposure during processing triggers HIPAA and HITECH liability. This checklist operationalizes the specific controls required to deploy a compliant, auditable, and clinically safe RAG system. Given failure consequences that include patient harm and fines up to $1.5M per violation, every item here is non-negotiable before go-live.

sections.heading: PHI Handling & Data Governance
sections.description: Ensure all patient health information is properly controlled throughout the RAG pipeline — from document ingestion through vector storage to LLM context assembly — in compliance with HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11.

items.label: PHI De-identification Before LLM Submission
items.description: Implement an automated de-identification layer (using NLP-based NER or rule-based redaction) that strips or replaces all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers from document chunks before they are assembled into LLM prompts. Validate with a secondary detection pass and log every redaction event.

items.label: Data Residency Enforcement
items.description: Configure all pipeline components — vector database, embedding model, LLM inference endpoint — to operate within a single, defined processing environment (e.g., on-premises or a HIPAA-eligible cloud region). Use network egress controls and VPC policies to block any patient data from leaving this boundary.

items.label: Vector Store Encryption at Rest and in Transit
items.description: Encrypt all vector embeddings and associated metadata using AES-256 at rest. Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit between pipeline components. Maintain encryption key management via a FIPS 140-2 validated KMS with documented key rotation schedules.

items.label: Business Associate Agreement (BAA) Coverage
items.description: Confirm executed BAAs are in place with every third-party vendor in the RAG stack, including cloud infrastructure providers, vector database vendors, and any managed LLM API providers. Document BAA scope and verify it explicitly covers AI processing workloads.

items.label: Document Ingestion Access Controls
items.description: Restrict document ingestion pipelines to authenticated, role-based service accounts. Implement attribute-based access control (ABAC) so that only documents matching a user's clinical role and patient relationship are retrievable during query time.

items.label: Minimum Necessary Standard Enforcement
items.description: Configure retrieval filters to return only the minimum necessary clinical context required to answer a query. Limit chunk size and metadata fields returned to exclude extraneous PHI not relevant to the specific clinical question being answered.

items.label: Data Retention and Deletion Policies
items.description: Define and enforce retention schedules for all pipeline artifacts: ingested documents, vector embeddings, query logs, and LLM prompt/response pairs. Implement automated deletion workflows and verify that deletion cascades across all storage layers including backups.

sections.heading: RAG Pipeline Accuracy & Hallucination Controls
sections.description: Clinical RAG outputs must be grounded in retrieved source documents. Implement technical controls to detect and suppress hallucinated content, verify source faithfulness, and ensure citations are accurate before responses reach clinical users.

items.label: Source Faithfulness Scoring
items.description: Integrate a faithfulness evaluation step (e.g., using an NLI model or dedicated faithfulness scorer) that compares each LLM-generated claim against the retrieved source chunks. Flag and quarantine responses where faithfulness score falls below a defined threshold (e.g., <0.85) before delivery to the clinician.

items.label: Hallucination Detection Gate
items.description: Deploy a secondary hallucination detection model or rule-based verifier that checks for factual claims in the LLM response that have no grounding in the retrieved context. Implement a hard block on responses containing ungrounded clinical assertions such as drug names, dosages, or diagnostic criteria.

items.label: Citation Accuracy Verification
items.description: For every source citation included in a RAG response, programmatically verify that the cited document ID exists in the knowledge base, the cited passage appears in the retrieved chunks, and the LLM's characterization of the source matches the actual source text. Surface citation confidence scores to end users.

items.label: Context Relevance Filtering
items.description: Implement a context relevance scoring step between retrieval and LLM synthesis. Use a cross-encoder reranker to score each retrieved chunk against the query and exclude chunks below a relevance threshold. Log excluded chunks for audit review.

items.label: Retrieval Recall Benchmarking
items.description: Establish a clinical QA benchmark dataset with known correct source documents. Measure retrieval recall@k (k=5 and k=10) monthly. Define a minimum acceptable recall threshold and trigger a pipeline review if performance drops below it.

items.label: Temporal Validity Checks on Retrieved Documents
items.description: Tag all ingested clinical documents with publication and last-reviewed dates. During retrieval, surface document age to the LLM and include a staleness warning in the response when retrieved documents exceed a defined recency threshold (e.g., clinical guidelines older than 2 years).

items.label: Confidence Score Display to Clinicians
items.description: Surface a composite confidence score to end users for every RAG response, derived from retrieval relevance, faithfulness, and citation accuracy sub-scores. Require clinicians to acknowledge low-confidence responses before acting on them.

sections.heading: Auditability & FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance
sections.description: All AI-assisted clinical decisions must be fully auditable per HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements. Implement immutable logging, electronic signature controls, and audit trail mechanisms across every stage of the RAG pipeline.

items.label: Immutable Audit Log for Every Query-Response Pair
items.description: Log every RAG query with a tamper-evident, append-only record that includes: timestamp, authenticated user ID, query text (post-de-identification), retrieved document IDs and chunk hashes, LLM model version, full response text, and all evaluation scores. Store logs in a write-once storage system compliant with 21 CFR Part 11 §11.10(e).

items.label: Electronic Signature Controls for Clinical Actions
items.description: Require electronic signatures (compliant with 21 CFR Part 11 §11.50) when a clinician takes a clinical action based on a RAG-generated recommendation. Signature records must link the specific RAG response ID, the action taken, and the clinician's identity and credentials.

items.label: Model Version and Prompt Template Versioning
items.description: Maintain a versioned registry of all LLM model checkpoints, embedding model versions, and prompt templates used in production. Every audit log entry must reference the exact versions active at query time to enable retrospective review of any clinical decision.

items.label: Access Audit Trails for Knowledge Base Documents
items.description: Log every document retrieval event with the document ID, user identity, query context, and timestamp. Generate monthly access reports and flag anomalous access patterns (e.g., bulk retrieval, off-hours access) for security review.

items.label: Human-in-the-Loop Override Logging
items.description: When a clinician overrides or rejects a RAG-generated recommendation, capture the override event with the clinician's stated reason, the original RAG response, and the alternative action taken. Use this data to identify systematic pipeline failures.

items.label: Audit Log Integrity Verification
items.description: Implement cryptographic hash chaining or a blockchain-anchored audit log to detect any tampering with historical records. Run automated integrity checks on audit logs daily and alert the compliance team on any hash mismatch.

items.label: Audit Log Retention for Minimum 6 Years
items.description: Retain all RAG pipeline audit logs for a minimum of 6 years per HIPAA requirements, or longer if state regulations require it. Ensure logs remain accessible and readable throughout the retention period, including after system upgrades.

sections.heading: Operational Safety & Incident Response
sections.description: Establish runtime safeguards, monitoring thresholds, and incident response procedures specific to a clinical RAG deployment. Ensure the system fails safely, escalates appropriately, and meets SOC 2 Type II operational controls.

items.label: Clinical Safety Guardrails on LLM Output
items.description: Implement hard-coded output filters that block LLM responses containing: specific medication dosage recommendations without physician review flags, diagnostic conclusions presented as definitive, or any content that could constitute unauthorized practice of medicine. Define these guardrails in a versioned policy document.

items.label: Fail-Safe Degradation Mode
items.description: Define and implement a fail-safe mode triggered when pipeline components are unavailable or evaluation scores fall below safety thresholds. In fail-safe mode, the system must surface a clear message to the clinician that AI assistance is unavailable and direct them to authoritative clinical resources.

items.label: Real-Time Anomaly Monitoring and Alerting
items.description: Deploy monitoring dashboards tracking: hallucination detection rate, faithfulness score distribution, retrieval latency, query volume anomalies, and de-identification failure rates. Configure PagerDuty or equivalent alerts for any metric crossing a defined safety threshold, with escalation to the clinical informatics team within 15 minutes.

items.label: HIPAA Breach Notification Workflow
items.description: Document and test a breach notification workflow that activates within 1 hour of detecting a PHI exposure event in the RAG pipeline. The workflow must include: containment steps, affected individual identification, HHS notification within 60 days, and media notification if >500 individuals are affected in a single state.

items.label: Penetration Testing of RAG Pipeline Endpoints
items.description: Conduct annual penetration testing specifically targeting RAG pipeline attack surfaces: prompt injection via malicious document ingestion, retrieval manipulation to surface incorrect clinical content, and embedding inversion attacks to reconstruct PHI from vector stores. Remediate all critical findings before the next deployment cycle.

items.label: Clinician Feedback Loop Integration
items.description: Implement an in-application feedback mechanism allowing clinicians to flag inaccurate, harmful, or irrelevant RAG responses. Route flagged responses to a clinical informatics reviewer within 24 hours. Track flag rates by query category and use them to trigger knowledge base updates or pipeline reconfigurations.

items.label: SOC 2 Type II Control Mapping for RAG Components
items.description: Map each RAG pipeline component (vector store, embedding service, LLM endpoint, orchestration layer) to the relevant SOC 2 Type II trust service criteria. Ensure each component is included in the annual SOC 2 audit scope and that control evidence (logs, configurations, access reviews) is collected continuously.

items.label: Disaster Recovery and RTO/RPO Validation
items.description: Define Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) for the RAG pipeline. Test failover to backup infrastructure quarterly. Ensure knowledge base snapshots are taken daily and can be restored within the defined RPO without data loss that would compromise clinical continuity.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR §164.514) requires that all 18 Safe Harbor identifiers be removed before PHI can be considered de-identified. For RAG pipelines, this applies not only to document chunks passed to the LLM but also to query text entered by users, which may inadvertently contain patient identifiers. Automated de-identification must operate bidirectionally on both inputs and outputs.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 applies when a RAG system is used to support decisions in FDA-regulated activities such as clinical trials, adverse event reporting, or drug safety monitoring. In these contexts, the electronic records generated by the RAG pipeline — including query logs, retrieved documents, and LLM responses — constitute electronic records subject to Part 11 controls including audit trails, access controls, and electronic signature requirements.

The HITECH Act (42 U.S.C. §17931) extends HIPAA obligations directly to Business Associates and introduces tiered civil penalties up to $1.5M per violation category per year. For RAG deployments, this means that any vendor providing vector database, embedding, or LLM services who has access to PHI must have an executed BAA and must independently demonstrate HIPAA compliance — the covered entity cannot transfer liability through contractual indemnification alone.

SOC 2 Type II certification requires continuous operational evidence over an audit period (typically 6-12 months), not just point-in-time controls. For a RAG pipeline, this means logging, access control enforcement, encryption, and incident response procedures must be consistently operational and evidenced throughout the audit window. Organizations should engage their auditor early to confirm that AI pipeline components are explicitly included in the audit scope, as many legacy SOC 2 frameworks do not address vector stores or LLM inference endpoints by default.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your RAG pipeline against HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 controls — catching PHI exposure, hallucination spikes, and audit gaps before they become violations.
